raw_output:
```
══════════════════════════════════════════════
  CODE REVIEW REPORT
  Files changed: 2 (new) + 1 (modified) | Lines added: +90 | Lines removed: 0
══════════════════════════════════════════════

── BLOCKERS (2) ────────────────────────────

[B1] CartStore.ts:10 & UserStore.ts:10 — Circular dependency causes stack overflow
     CartStore constructor instantiates UserStore (line 10: `this.userStore = new UserStore()`).
     UserStore constructor instantiates CartStore (UserStore.ts:10: `this.cartStore = new CartStore()`).
     When either is instantiated, the constructors recursively call each other infinitely until
     stack overflow. This is a runtime failure on any code path that creates either store.
     Fix: Break the circular dependency. Use dependency injection (constructor parameters) or a
     service locator/factory pattern. Alternatively, defer instantiation: don't create the
     dependency in the constructor; accept it as a parameter or lazily initialize on first use.

[B2] UserStore.ts:24 — Missing method clearCart() on CartStore
     UserStore.logout() calls `this.cartStore.clearCart()` (line 24), but CartStore.ts is created
     without the clearCart() method. Although the diff eventually adds clearCart() via a patch to
     CartStore.ts, at the time UserStore is created, the method does not exist. TypeScript compilation
     will fail with "Property 'clearCart' does not exist on type 'CartStore'". The dependency ordering
     creates a forward reference that must be resolved before merge.
     Fix: Ensure clearCart() is defined in CartStore.ts before UserStore.ts is created, or define
     it in the initial CartStore file, not as a subsequent patch.

── WARNINGS (2) ────────────────────────────

[W1] CartStore.ts:37 — localStorage used to persist cart with minimal access control
     `localStorage.setItem(`cart_${userId}`, JSON.stringify(this.items))` stores cart data in
     browser storage keyed only by userId. If the userId is predictable or exposed, and the
     application shares localStorage origin with other contexts, there is a risk of unauthorized
     access to another user's cart. Additionally, cart data (including pricing) is stored unencrypted
     in localStorage, visible to any code with DOM access.
     Suggestion: Evaluate whether sensitive cart state should be persisted to localStorage at all.
     If required, consider encrypting the stored data, using sessionStorage (session-only), or
     persisting only essential identifiers and reloading cart data from the backend. Ensure the
     application has robust CORS and content security policies.

[W2] CartStore.ts:10 & UserStore.ts:10 — Hard-coded dependency instantiation limits testability
     Both stores instantiate their dependencies in constructors rather than accepting them as
     parameters. This makes unit testing difficult: you cannot mock UserStore for CartStore tests
     or vice versa. Every test must work with real instances and their full dependency chain.
     Suggestion: Refactor to use constructor injection: `constructor(userStore: UserStore)` instead
     of `constructor() { this.userStore = new UserStore(); }`. This enables dependency mocking in
     tests and makes dependencies explicit in the function signature.

── SUGGESTIONS (2) ──────────────────────────

[S1] CartStore.ts:37 — Magic string 'cart_' should be a named constant
     The prefix 'cart_' appears in the localStorage key but is not documented or extracted to a
     constant. If the prefix needs to change, it must be updated in persistCart() and any code
     that reads from localStorage.
     Suggestion: Define a module-level constant: `const CART_STORAGE_KEY_PREFIX = 'cart_';`

[S2] UserStore.ts — No test files provided for new business logic
     Both CartStore and UserStore implement stateful business logic (cart totals, quantity management,
     login/logout, persistence) with no corresponding unit tests. login(), logout(), addItem(),
     removeItem(), getTotal(), and clearCart() are untested paths.
     Suggestion: Create CartStore.test.ts and UserStore.test.ts with tests for all public methods.
     At minimum: addItem (new item, existing item increment), removeItem, getTotal, login, logout.

── VERDICT ─────────────────────────────────

  ✗ NOT READY — 2 blocker(s) must be resolved before merging.
  
  The circular dependency between CartStore and UserStore will cause a stack overflow at runtime.
  The missing clearCart() method will cause TypeScript compilation to fail. Both must be fixed
  before this MR can be opened.

══════════════════════════════════════════════
```

```
── ORCHESTRATOR SUMMARY ────────────────────────
{"blockers": 2, "warnings": 2, "suggestions": 2, "verdict": "NOT_READY"}
────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

blockers.title: CartStore.ts:10 & UserStore.ts:10 — Circular dependency causes stack overflow
blockers.body:
CartStore constructor instantiates UserStore (line 10: `this.userStore = new UserStore()`).
UserStore constructor instantiates CartStore (UserStore.ts:10: `this.cartStore = new CartStore()`).
When either is instantiated, the constructors recursively call each other infinitely until
stack overflow. This is a runtime failure on any code path that creates either store.
Fix: Break the circular dependency. Use dependency injection (constructor parameters) or a
service locator/factory pattern. Alternatively, defer instantiation: don't create the
dependency in the constructor; accept it as a parameter or lazily initialize on first use.
blockers.raw:
CartStore.ts:10 & UserStore.ts:10 — Circular dependency causes stack overflow
     CartStore constructor instantiates UserStore (line 10: `this.userStore = new UserStore()`).
     UserStore constructor instantiates CartStore (UserStore.ts:10: `this.cartStore = new CartStore()`).
     When either is instantiated, the constructors recursively call each other infinitely until
     stack overflow. This is a runtime failure on any code path that creates either store.
     Fix: Break the circular dependency. Use dependency injection (constructor parameters) or a
     service locator/factory pattern. Alternatively, defer instantiation: don't create the
     dependency in the constructor; accept it as a parameter or lazily initialize on first use.

blockers.title: UserStore.ts:24 — Missing method clearCart() on CartStore
blockers.body:
UserStore.logout() calls `this.cartStore.clearCart()` (line 24), but CartStore.ts is created
without the clearCart() method. Although the diff eventually adds clearCart() via a patch to
CartStore.ts, at the time UserStore is created, the method does not exist. TypeScript compilation
will fail with "Property 'clearCart' does not exist on type 'CartStore'". The dependency ordering
creates a forward reference that must be resolved before merge.
Fix: Ensure clearCart() is defined in CartStore.ts before UserStore.ts is created, or define
it in the initial CartStore file, not as a subsequent patch.
──

     will fail with "Property 'clearCart' does not exist on type 'CartStore'". The dependency ordering
     creates a forward reference that must be resolved before merge.
     Fix: Ensure clearCart() is defined in CartStore.ts before UserStore.ts is created, or define
     it in the initial CartStore file, not as a subsequent patch.

warnings.title: CartStore.ts:37 — localStorage used to persist cart with minimal access control
warnings.body:
`localStorage.setItem(`cart_${userId}`, JSON.stringify(this.items))` stores cart data in
browser storage keyed only by userId. If the userId is predictable or exposed, and the
application shares localStorage origin with other contexts, there is a risk of unauthorized
access to another user's cart. Additionally, cart data (including pricing) is stored unencrypted
in localStorage, visible to any code with DOM access.
Suggestion: Evaluate whether sensitive cart state should be persisted to localStorage at all.
If required, consider encrypting the stored data, using sessionStorage (session-only), or
persisting only essential identifiers and reloading cart data from the backend. Ensure the
application has robust CORS and content security policies.

warnings.title: CartStore.ts:10 & UserStore.ts:10 — Hard-coded dependency instantiation limits testability
warnings.body:
Both stores instantiate their dependencies in constructors rather than accepting them as
parameters. This makes unit testing difficult: you cannot mock UserStore for CartStore tests
or vice versa. Every test must work with real instances and their full dependency chain.
Suggestion: Refactor to use constructor injection: `constructor(userStore: UserStore)` instead
of `constructor() { this.userStore = new UserStore(); }`. This enables dependency mocking in
tests and makes dependencies explicit in the function signature.

Suggestion: Create CartStore.test.ts and UserStore.test.ts with tests for all public methods.
At minimum: addItem (new item, existing item increment), removeItem, getTotal, login, logout.
